items.q: What is the difference between mitigation and adaptation to climate change?
items.correct: Mitigation reduces the causes (cutting greenhouse gas emissions); adaptation reduces the impacts (e.g. building sea walls, drought-resistant crops, early warning systems).
items.misconception: Mitigation and adaptation mean the same thing — both are about stopping climate change.
items.random_wrong: Mitigation is done by HICs and adaptation is done by LICs.
items.why: The distinction is causes vs consequences. Both are needed: mitigation tackles the source; adaptation copes with already-locked-in change. Many LICs adapt because they can't mitigate global emissions alone.

items.q: What causes global sea level rise?
items.correct: Two main factors: thermal expansion of warming seawater, and the melting of land-based ice (glaciers and ice sheets) adding water to oceans.
items.misconception: Sea level rises mainly because Arctic sea ice is melting.
items.random_wrong: Sea level rises because of underwater volcanoes pushing it up.
items.why: Floating sea ice already displaces its weight in water — its melting does not raise sea level. The contributors are LAND ice (Greenland, Antarctica) and thermal expansion.

items.q: What is the enhanced greenhouse effect?
items.correct: The strengthening of the natural greenhouse effect by human activities (mainly burning fossil fuels) which add CO2 and other gases to the atmosphere.
items.misconception: The greenhouse effect is entirely man-made and harmful.
items.random_wrong: It is the hole in the ozone layer letting in more sun.
items.why: The natural greenhouse effect is essential for life — without it Earth would be -18 degrees C. The problem is enhancement. Students also confuse it with the (separate) ozone hole.

items.q: Which evidence source shows CO₂ levels going back 800,000 years?
items.why: It's Ice cores. “Tree rings” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is an example of climate change ADAPTATION?
items.why: It's Building flood defences. “Switching to solar power” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: By what year does the UK aim to achieve net-zero emissions?
items.why: Exact answer: 2050. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
